questionText: If the height of a pole is $2\sqrt{3}$ metres and the length of its shadow is $2$ metres, find the angle of elevation of the sun.

steps.explanation: Let AB be the pole and AC be its shadow. The angle of elevation of the sun is ∠ACB = θ.
Compute tan θ using the ratio of pole height to shadow length.
Since tan 60° = √3, the angle of elevation is 60°.

solution.shortcut: Recognise standard values: tan 30° = 1/√3, tan 45° = 1, tan 60° = √3.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which side is opposite and which is adjacent; here the shadow is the adjacent side and the pole is opposite.

modelName: Angle of Elevation – Pole and Shadow

questionText: A ladder leaning against a wall makes an angle of $60°$ with the ground. If the length of the ladder is $19$ m, find the distance of the foot of the ladder from the wall.

steps.explanation: Let AB be the wall, BC be the ladder of length 19 m. The angle ∠ACB = 60°. Let AC = x (distance of foot from wall).
Use cos 60° = 1/2.

solution.shortcut: Distance of foot = ladder length × cos(angle with ground).
solution.commonMistake: Using sin instead of cos for the horizontal base.

questionText: The angle of elevation of the top of a tower at a point on the ground is $30°$. On walking $24$ m towards the tower, the angle of elevation becomes $60°$. Find the height of the tower.

steps.explanation: Let AB = h (height of tower), D be the farther point, C be the closer point with CD = 24 m. From D: tan 30° = h/AD, so AD = h√3.
From C: tan 60° = h/AC, so AC = h/√3.
CD = AD − AC = h√3 − h/√3 = h(3−1)/√3 = 2h/√3. Set this equal to 24.
Compute numerical value: h = 12√3 = 12 × 1.732 ≈ 20.76 m.

solution.shortcut: When angles are 30° and 60°, height = (distance walked × √3)/2.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that the distance walked is the difference of the two base distances, not the sum.

questionText: A man standing on the bank of a river observes that the angle subtended by a tree on the opposite bank is $60°$. When he retires $36$ m from the bank, he finds the angle to be $30°$. Find the breadth of the river.

steps.explanation: Let AB = h (height of tree), AC = x (breadth of river), C is the first position and D is 36 m back. AD = 36 + x.
From D: tan 30° = h/AD ⇒ h = (36+x)/√3.
From C: tan 60° = h/x ⇒ h = x√3.

solution.shortcut: Breadth = distance retired / (cot(nearer angle) − cot(farther angle)).

questionText: A man on the top of a tower, standing on the seashore, finds that a boat coming towards him takes $10$ minutes for the angle of depression to change from $30°$ to $60°$. Find the time taken by the boat to reach the shore from this position.

steps.explanation: Let AB = h (tower height), D be the far position (angle of depression 30°), C be the near position (angle of depression 60°), and A be the base of the tower. Let AD = y, CD = x.
Angle of depression = angle of elevation from boat. tan 60° = h/y ⇒ y = h/√3.
Boat covers x = 2h/√3 in 10 minutes. Remaining distance to shore = y = h/√3.

solution.shortcut: Remaining distance y = x/2, so time = 10/2 = 5 minutes.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing distance covered (CD) with remaining distance (AD).

modelName: Boat Approaching Shore – Time from Tower

formulas.title: Angle of depression equals angle of elevation

questionText: There are two temples, one on each bank of a river, just opposite to each other. One temple is $54$ m high. From the top of this temple, the angles of depression of the top and the foot of the other temple are $30°$ and $60°$ respectively. Find the width of the river and the height of the other temple.
correctAnswer: Width = $18\sqrt{3}$ m ≈ 31.14 m; Height of other temple = 36 m

steps.explanation: Let AB = 54 m (taller temple), CD = h (shorter temple), AC = x (river width). Angle of depression of foot C from top B is 60°, so tan 60° = AB/AC.
DE is horizontal from D to the taller temple. DE = AC = 18√3. BE = AB − CD = 54 − h. Angle of depression of top D from B is 30°, so tan 30° = BE/DE.
Height of shorter temple CD = AB − BE = 54 − 18 = 36 m.
Numerical width: 18√3 = 18 × 1.732 ≈ 31.14 m.

solution.shortcut: Use angle of depression to foot to find width; then use angle of depression to top to find height difference.
solution.commonMistake: Using angle of depression to top to find width instead of angle to foot.

modelName: Two Temples on River Banks – Width and Height
